texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Measurement of Length and Motion
texts.text: Measurement of Length and Motion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Deepa, a curious eleven-year old girl, livesina town in the state of Haryana. The new school year has started. Deepa needs a new uniform since she has grown taller. Her mother takes her to a cloth shop. She asks for a two-metre cloth piece. The shopkeeper measures the cloth using a metal measuring rod.
texts.text: Deepa, a curious eleven-year old girl, livesina town in the state of Haryana. The new school year has started. Deepa needs a new uniform since she has grown taller. Her mother takes her to a cloth shop. She asks for a two-metre cloth piece. The shopkeeper measures the cloth using a metal measuring rod.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Then, the tailor takes her measurements using a flexible measuring tape. Her mother instructs the tailor to increase the length ofher uniform by char angula (four fingers width).
texts.text: Then, the tailor takes her measurements using a flexible measuring tape. Her mother instructs the tailor to increase the length ofher uniform by char angula (four fingers width).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Are the tape and rod similar to the scale that the elder sister has in her geometry box? What did mother mean by char angula?
texts.text: Are the tape and rod similar to the scale that the elder sister has in her geometry box? What did mother mean by char angula?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Deepa shares her experience with her school friends Anish, Hardeep, Padma, Tasneem and this leads to a discussion amongst them.
texts.text: Deepa shares her experience with her school friends Anish, Hardeep, Padma, Tasneem and this leads to a discussion amongst them.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5] How do we Measure?
texts.text: 5] How do we Measure?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hardeep says, "I have seen my grandmother measuring cloth by the length of her arm."
texts.text: Hardeep says, "I have seen my grandmother measuring cloth by the length of her arm."

texts.label: text
texts.orig: "Have you ever seen how a farmer measures length to divide his field into beds? He walks and counts the number of his strides," says Padma.
texts.text: "Have you ever seen how a farmer measures length to divide his field into beds? He walks and counts the number of his strides," says Padma.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: "Oh, not just the length of the strides—sometimes they also use the length of their feet to measure," adds Anish.
texts.text: "Oh, not just the length of the strides—sometimes they also use the length of their feet to measure," adds Anish.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Deepa says excitedly, "Measuring length using body parts must be so much fun! Let us also measure something using a body part."
texts.text: Deepa says excitedly, "Measuring length using body parts must be so much fun! Let us also measure something using a body part."

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 5.1: Use ofhandspan for measuring
texts.text: Fig. 5.1: Use ofhandspan for measuring

texts.label: text
texts.orig: "What should we measure? Okay, let us measure the length ofthe tablein our classroom," says Tasneem.
texts.text: "What should we measure? Okay, let us measure the length ofthe table in our classroom," says Tasneem.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Padma adds, "And which body part should we use to measure it?"
texts.text: Padma adds, "And which body part should we use to measure it?"

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Deepa says, "Let us use our handspan. I will show you how to use it. Ihave seen my mother using it. She calls it balisht."
texts.text: Deepa says, "Let us use our handspan. I will show you how to use it. Ihave seen my mother using it. She calls it balisht."

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hardeep adds, "Okay. Let us also note down our measurements."
texts.text: Hardeep adds, "Okay. Let us also note down our measurements."

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Tasneem
texts.text: Tasneem

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Padma says, "Oh, the number of handspans is different for all of us. So, what can we say about the length of the table?"
texts.text: Padma says, "Oh, the number of handspans is different for all of us. So, what can we say about the length of the table?"

texts.label: text
texts.orig: "But why should the number be different?" Hardeep asked thoughtfully.
texts.text: "But why should the number be different?" Hardeep asked thoughtfully.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Tasneem says, "I can guess. Our handspans are of different sizes."
texts.text: Tasneem says, "I can guess. Our handspans are of different sizes."

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Anish gives an idea, "Let us check this."
texts.text: Anish gives an idea, "Let us check this."

texts.label: text
texts.orig: So, all five of them put their handspans along each other and arrive at the conclusion that the lengths of their handspans are different.
texts.text: So, all five of them put their handspans along each other and arrive at the conclusion that the lengths of their handspans are different.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Deepa says thoughtfully, "No wonder people use scales and measuring tapes."
texts.text: Deepa says thoughtfully, "No wonder people use scales and measuring tapes."

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Deepa and her friends compare the length of the table with the length of their handspans. The length of the table is expressed in terms of their handspans. Here, the handspan used for measurement is an example ofa unit. And the length is expressed in two parts, anumber and a unit. For example, if the length of the table is found to be 13 handspans, then 13 is the number and 'handspan' is the unit selected for the measurement.
texts.text: Deepa and her friends compare the length of the table with the length of their handspans. The length of the table is expressed in terms of their handspans. Here, the handspan used for measurement is an example ofa unit. And the length is expressed in two parts, anumber and a unit. For example, if the length of the table is found to be 13 handspans, then 13 is the number and 'handspan' is the unit selected for the measurement.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, handspans and other similar units, such as length of hand, foot, fist or fingers, differ from person to person. Thus, there is a need for such a unit for which measurements of the same length made by different people do not differ.
texts.text: However, handspans and other similar units, such as length of hand, foot, fist or fingers, differ from person to person. Thus, there is a need for such a unit for which measurements of the same length made by different people do not differ.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5 2 Standard Units
texts.text: 5 2 Standard Units

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several systems of units evolved with time in different parts of the world. However, when people started travelling from one place to another, it created a lot of confusion. This led to the different countries coming together and adopting a set of standard units of measurement. The system of units now used is known as the 'International System of Units' or SI units.
texts.text: Several systems of units evolved with time in different parts of the world. However, when people started travelling from one place to another, it created a lot of confusion. This led to the different countries coming together and adopting a set of standard units of measurement. The system of units now used is known as the 'International System of Units' or SI units.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The SI unit of length is metre. Its symbol is m. A metre scale is shown in Fig. 5.2. One metre (m) is divided into 100 equal divisions. Each division is called a centimetre (cm). You may be familiar with a smaller part of the metre scale, typically 15 cm long, shown in Fig. 5.3.
texts.text: The SI unit of length is metre. Its symbol is m. A metre scale is shown in Fig. 5.2. One metre (m) is divided into 100 equal divisions. Each division is called a centimetre (cm). You may be familiar with a smaller part of the metre scale, typically 15 cm long, shown in Fig. 5.3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Look carefully at the 15-cm scale. It has markings (in cm) from 0 to 15. The length of any section between two consecutive big marks, such as between 1 and 2 or between 5 and 6, is 1 cm. Observe that these sections
texts.text: Look carefully at the 15-cm scale. It has markings (in cm) from 0 to 15. The length of any section between two consecutive big marks, such as between 1 and 2 or between 5 and 6, is 1 cm. Observe that these sections

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 5.2: A metre scale
texts.text: Fig. 5.2: A metre scale

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 5.3: A 15-cm scale
texts.text: Fig. 5.3: A 15-cm scale

texts.label: text
texts.orig: of 1 cm length are further divided into 10 equal parts. The length of one of these smaller parts is called a millimetre (mm). 1 mm is the smallest value of length that you can measure using this scale. 1 mm is equal to one-tenth of a centimeter (1 mm = 0.1 cm).
texts.text: of 1 cm length are further divided into 10 equal parts. The length of one of these smaller parts is called a millimetre (mm). 1 mm is the smallest value of length that you can measure using this scale. 1 mm is equal to one-tenth of a centimeter (1 mm = 0.1 cm).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For measuring larger lengths, we use a larger unit called a kilometre (km) which is equal to 1000 metres. And for measuring smaller lengths, we use units such as centimetre or millimetre.
texts.text: For measuring larger lengths, we use a larger unit called a kilometre (km) which is equal to 1000 metres. And for measuring smaller lengths, we use units such as centimetre or millimetre.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Would it be convenient to use the unit metre to measure larger lengths, such as the length of a railway track between two cities,orto @ measure smaller lengths, such as the thickness of a page of a book?
texts.text: Would it be convenient to use the unit metre to measure larger lengths, such as the length of a railway track between two cities, or to measure smaller lengths, such as the thickness of a page of a book?

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5.3 Correct Way of Measuring Length
texts.text: 5.3 Correct Way of Measuring Length

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For measuring any length, we need an appropriate scale. For example, if you want to measure the length of your pencil, you may use a 15-cm scale. Similarly, if the height of a room is to be measured, you may need a metre scale or a measuring tape. You cannot directly measure the girth of a tree or the size of your chest using a metre scale. For such measurements, flexible measuring tape, such as a tailor's tape is more suitable.
texts.text: For measuring any length, we need an appropriate scale. For example, if you want to measure the length of your pencil, you may use a 15-cm scale. Similarly, if the height of a room is to be measured, you may need a metre scale or a measuring tape. You cannot directly measure the girth of a tree or the size of your chest using a metre scale. For such measurements, flexible measuring tape, such as a tailor's tape is more suitable.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While measuring lengths, we need to take care of some points.
texts.text: While measuring lengths, we need to take care of some points.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: What is the correct way to place the scale?
texts.text: What is the correct way to place the scale?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Place the scale in contact with the object along its length as shown in Fig. 5.4.
texts.text: Place the scale in contact with the object along its length as shown in Fig. 5.4.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 5.4: Method of placing the scale
texts.text: Fig. 5.4: Method of placing the scale

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: (b) Incorrect
texts.text: (b) Incorrect

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: What is the correct position of the eye while reading the scale?
texts.text: What is the correct position of the eye while reading the scale?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For example, if you are trying to measure the length of a pencil by aligning it with a scale, the position of your eye should be directly above the tip of the pencil (Fig. 5.5).
texts.text: For example, if you are trying to measure the length of a pencil by aligning it with a scale, the position of your eye should be directly above the tip of the pencil (Fig. 5.5).

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 5.5: Correct position of the eye is 'B'
texts.text: Fig. 5.5: Correct position of the eye is 'B'

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: How to measure the length if the ends of the scale are broken?
texts.text: How to measure the length if the ends of the scale are broken?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If the ends of the scale are broken or the zero marking is not clear, it can still be used for measurement. With such a scale, use any other full mark of the scale, say, 1.0 cm (Fig. 5.6). Then you must subtract the reading of this mark from the reading at the other end. For example, in Fig. 5.6, the reading at one end is 1.0 cm and at the other end, it is 10.4 cm. Therefore, the length of the object is 10.4 cm-1.0 cm = 9.4 cm.
texts.text: If the ends of the scale are broken or the zero marking is not clear, it can still be used for measurement. With such a scale, use any other full mark of the scale, say, 1.0 cm (Fig. 5.6). Then you must subtract the reading of this mark from the reading at the other end. For example, in Fig. 5.6, the reading at one end is 1.0 cm and at the other end, it is 10.4 cm. Therefore, the length of the object is 10.4 cm-1.0 cm = 9.4 cm.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 5.6: Correct method of placing the scale with broken end
texts.text: Fig. 5.6: Correct method of placing the scale with broken end

texts.text: Activity 5.1: Let us measure

texts.text: @ Select some objects around you, such as a comb, a pen, a pencil, and an eraser to measure their lengths.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Measure their lengths one by one using a metre scale and note down the measurements in Table 5.2.
texts.text: ¢ Measure their lengths one by one using a metre scale and note down the measurements in Table 5.2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While writing the length, do not forget to write the unit also. Thus, your result will consist of two parts—one part is a number and the other part is the unit of measurement.
texts.text: While writing the length, do not forget to write the unit also. Thus, your result will consist of two parts—one part is a number and the other part is the unit of measurement.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some of your friends in the class would have measured the length of the same objects. Compare the lengths measured by you with that of your friends. Are the measured lengths the same or slightly different? If not the same, discuss the possible reasons for the differences.
texts.text: Some of your friends in the class would have measured the length of the same objects. Compare the lengths measured by you with that of your friends. Are the measured lengths the same or slightly different? If not the same, discuss the possible reasons for the differences.

texts.text: 5.4 Measuring the Length of a Curved Line

texts.orig: In the case of a curved line, measurements can be made with the help of a flexible measuring tape or by using a thread as shown in Fig. 5.8.
texts.text: In the case of a curved line, measurements can be made with the help of a flexible measuring tape or by using a thread as shown in Fig. 5.8.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5.5 Describing Position
texts.text: 5.5 Describing Position

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One day the teacher informs her students that she has planned an educational visit to a nearby garden. She asks the students to reach there directly in the morning. Deepa and her friends start discussing whether the garden would be closer than their school or farther. Tasneem and Padma say that the garden would be closer, while Deepa and Anish feel that the school would be closer, Hardeep thinks that both would be almost at an equal distance (Fig. 5.9).
texts.text: One day the teacher informs her students that she has planned an educational visit to a nearby garden. She asks the students to reach there directly in the morning. Deepa and her friends start discussing whether the garden would be closer than their school or farther. Tasneem and Padma say that the garden would be closer, while Deepa and Anish feel that the school would be closer, Hardeep thinks that both would be almost at an equal distance (Fig. 5.9).

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 5.9: Location of bus stand, school, garden and houses of Deepa and her friends
texts.text: Fig. 5.9: Location of bus stand, school, garden and houses of Deepa and her friends

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Who do you think is correct? All of them are correct (Fig. 5.9). Then, why are their observations different? They are locating the distances of the school and garden from their houses. If, instead, each of them had thought of distances from a same object or point, say, the bus stand, then their observations would have been the same.
texts.text: Who do you think is correct? All of them are correct (Fig. 5.9). Then, why are their observations different? They are locating the distances of the school and garden from their houses. If, instead, each of them had thought of distances from a same object or point, say, the bus stand, then their observations would have been the same.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When distance is stated with respect to a fixed object or point, then this point is called a reference point.
texts.text: When distance is stated with respect to a fixed object or point, then this point is called a reference point.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A few days later, Hardeep tells his friends excitedly, "Let us all go to the playground. The sports teacher wants us to help her to draw lines with chuna powder (limestone powder) for making the Kabaddi court for the sports day."
texts.text: A few days later, Hardeep tells his friends excitedly, "Let us all go to the playground. The sports teacher wants us to help her to draw lines with chuna powder (limestone powder) for making the Kabaddi court for the sports day."

texts.text: Padma: "We will need a longer measuring tape. Let us take it from the sports room." (Fig. 5.10)

texts.text: Deepa: "Let us first decide the point on the ground from which we will measure the distances to start drawing the lines. Let us call this our reference point." (Fig. 5.11)

texts.orig: Pig. 3.12: A kilometre stone
texts.text: Pig. 3.12: A kilometre stone

texts.label: text
texts.orig: After a few days, Padma was travelling by bus to visit her grandparents in Delhi. She was eager to reach Delhi and was reading the kilometre stones on the side of the road. On one of the kilometre stones, it was written 'Delhi 70 km' (Fig. 5.12).
texts.text: After a few days, Padma was travelling by bus to visit her grandparents in Delhi. She was eager to reach Delhi and was reading the kilometre stones on the side of the road. On one of the kilometre stones, it was written 'Delhi 70 km' (Fig. 5.12).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Further on, the next kilometre stone read 'Delhi 60 km'. Each kilometre stone indicated that she was getting closer to her grandparents' house.
texts.text: Further on, the next kilometre stone read 'Delhi 60 km'. Each kilometre stone indicated that she was getting closer to her grandparents' house.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 5.13: Positions of kilometre stones with respect to Delhi as a reference point
texts.text: Fig. 5.13: Positions of kilometre stones with respect to Delhi as a reference point

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If the kilometre stone reads 'Delhi 70 km' as shown in Fig. 5.13, we can say that the position of Padma is 70 km from Delhi. When the kilometre stone reads 'Delhi 60 km', the position of Padma is 60 km from Delhi.
texts.text: If the kilometre stone reads 'Delhi 70 km' as shown in Fig. 5.13, we can say that the position of Padma is 70 km from Delhi. When the kilometre stone reads 'Delhi 60 km', the position of Padma is 60 km from Delhi.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Does this mean that the position of Padma, with respect to the reference point, is changing with time? When does the position of an object change with respect to a reference point? Does it change when an object is moving?
texts.text: Does this mean that the position of Padma, with respect to the reference point, is changing with time? When does the position of an object change with respect to a reference point? Does it change when an object is moving?

texts.orig: 5.6 Voving Things

texts.text: Activity 5.2: Let us explore

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Look around and prepare a list of five objects that are in motion and five objects that are at rest.
texts.text: ¢ Look around and prepare a list of five objects that are in motion and five objects that are at rest.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Record your observations in Table 5.3.
texts.text: ¢ Record your observations in Table 5.3.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Think about how you decided whether an object was in motion or at rest. Write your explanation (justification) in Table 5.3.
texts.text: ¢ Think about how you decided whether an object was in motion or at rest. Write your explanation (justification) in Table 5.3.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Table 5.3: Observing things around you
texts.text: Table 5.3: Observing things around you

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Compare and analyse your justifications. How can one decide if an object is in motion or at rest?
texts.text: Compare and analyse your justifications. How can one decide if an object is in motion or at rest?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An object is said to be in motion if its position changes with respect to the reference point with time. If an object is not changing its position with respect to the reference point with time, it is said to be at rest.
texts.text: An object is said to be in motion if its position changes with respect to the reference point with time. If an object is not changing its position with respect to the reference point with time, it is said to be at rest.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Deepa looked around her in the bus and noticed that all the passengers were seated. She looked around again after a minute and found them still occupying their seats. She wondered, 'Are they moving?' She concluded that the position of the passengers was not changing with time. Therefore, they were certainly at rest. However, when she looked outside, she felt they were in motion as their positions were changing with respect to things outside.
texts.text: Deepa looked around her in the bus and noticed that all the passengers were seated. She looked around again after a minute and found them still occupying their seats. She wondered, 'Are they moving?' She concluded that the position of the passengers was not changing with time. Therefore, they were certainly at rest. However, when she looked outside, she felt they were in motion as their positions were changing with respect to things outside.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The reference point is important in deciding whether an object is at rest or in motion. If Deepa considered herself (or the bus) as the reference point, then the passengers were at rest. However, if she considered any object J outside the bus (say a building) as the reference point, then the passengers (and the bus) were in motion. More
texts.text: The reference point is important in deciding whether an object is at rest or in motion. If Deepa considered herself (or the bus) as the reference point, then the passengers were at rest. However, if she considered any object J outside the bus (say a building) as the reference point, then the passengers (and the bus) were in motion. More

Recall the march-past of students during the parade. Do they move on a straight-line path? When a heavy box is pushed, it may also move along a straight line (Fig. 5.14).
texts.text: Does it move along a straight line? When an orange drops from the tree, does it move in a straight line? Have you seen the Republic Day parade? Recall the march-past of students during the parade. Do they move on a straight-line path? When a heavy box is pushed, it may also move along a straight line (Fig. 5.14).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When an object moves along a straight line, its motion is called linear motion. Identify such linear motion in your surroundings.
texts.text: When an object moves along a straight line, its motion is called linear motion. Identify such linear motion in your surroundings.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: The International System of Units (SI units) has been adopted by countries as standard units of measurement.
texts.text: The International System of Units (SI units) has been adopted by countries as standard units of measurement.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ The SI unit of length is metre. Its symbol is m.
texts.text: ¢ The SI unit of length is metre. Its symbol is m.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: When distance is stated with respect to a fixed object or point, then this point is called a reference point.
texts.text: When distance is stated with respect to a fixed object or point, then this point is called a reference point.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: An object is said to be in motion if its position changes with respect to a reference point with time.
texts.text: An object is said to be in motion if its position changes with respect to a reference point with time.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: When an object moves along a straight line, its motion is called linear motion.
texts.text: When an object moves along a straight line, its motion is called linear motion.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: When an object moves along a circular path, its motion is called circular motion.
texts.text: When an object moves along a circular path, its motion is called circular motion.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: When any object moves to and fro about any fixed position, its motion is called oscillatory motion.
texts.text: When any object moves to and fro about any fixed position, its motion is called oscillatory motion.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Some lengths are given in Column I of Table 5.5. Some units are given in Column II. Match the lengths with the units suitable for measuring those lengths.
texts.text: Some lengths are given in Column I of Table 5.5. Some units are given in Column II. Match the lengths with the units suitable for measuring those lengths.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Read the following statements and mark True (T) or False (F) against each.
texts.text: Read the following statements and mark True (T) or False (F) against each.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (i) The motion of a car moving on a straight road is an example of linear motion. [ ]
texts.text: (i) The motion of a car moving on a straight road is an example of linear motion. [ ]

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (ii) Any object which is changing its position with respect to a reference point with time is said to be in motion.
texts.text: (ii) Any object which is changing its position with respect to a reference point with time is said to be in motion.

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: (iii) 1km=100cm [ ]

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Which of the following is not a standard unit of measuring length?
texts.text: Which of the following is not a standard unit of measuring length?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (i) millimetre (ii) centimetre (iii) kilometre (iv) handspan
texts.text: (i) millimetre (ii) centimetre (iii) kilometre (iv) handspan

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Search for the different scales or measuring tapes at your home and school. Find out the smallest value that can be measured using each of these scales. Record your observations in a tabular form.
texts.text: Search for the different scales or measuring tapes at your home and school. Find out the smallest value that can be measured using each of these scales. Record your observations in a tabular form.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Suppose the distance between your school and home is 1.5 km. Express it in metres.
texts.text: Suppose the distance between your school and home is 1.5 km. Express it in metres.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Take a tumbler or a bottle. Measure the length of the curved part of the base of glass or bottle and record it.
texts.text: Take a tumbler or a bottle. Measure the length of the curved part of the base of glass or bottle and record it.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Measure the height of your friend and express it in (i) metres (ii) centimetres and (iii) millimetres.
texts.text: Measure the height of your friend and express it in (i) metres (ii) centimetres and (iii) millimetres.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: You are given a coin. Estimate how many coins are required to be placed one after the other lengthwise, without leaving any gap between them, to cover the whole length of the chosen side of a notebook. Verify your estimate by measuring the same side of the notebook and the size of the coin using a 15-cm scale.
texts.text: You are given a coin. Estimate how many coins are required to be placed one after the other lengthwise, without leaving any gap between them, to cover the whole length of the chosen side of a notebook. Verify your estimate by measuring the same side of the notebook and the size of the coin using a 15-cm scale.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Give two examples each for linear, circular and oscillatory motion.
texts.text: Give two examples each for linear, circular and oscillatory motion.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 10. Observe different objects around you. It is easier to express the lengths of some objects in mm, some in cm and some in m. Make a list of three objects in each category and enter them in the Table 5.6.
texts.text: Observe different objects around you. It is easier to express the lengths of some objects in mm, some in cm and some in m. Make a list of three objects in each category and enter them in the Table 5.6.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 11. A rollercoaster track is made in the shape shown in Fig. 5.19. A ball starts from point A and escapes through point F. Identify the types of motion of the ball on the rollercoaster and corresponding portions of the track.
texts.text: A rollercoaster track is made in the shape shown in Fig. 5.19. A ball starts from point A and escapes through point F. Identify the types of motion of the ball on the rollercoaster and corresponding portions of the track.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 12. Tasneem wants to make a metre scale by herself. She considers the following materials for it—plywood, paper, cloth, stretchable rubber and steel. Which of these should she not use and why?
texts.text: Tasneem wants to make a metre scale by herself. She considers the following materials for it—plywood, paper, cloth, stretchable rubber and steel. Which of these should she not use and why?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 13. Think, design and develop a card game on conversion of units of length to play with your friends.
texts.text: Think, design and develop a card game on conversion of units of length to play with your friends.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Can you find the thickness of a single page of your notebook or textbook using a scale? Think of a way and write it. Carry out the activity and report your result.
texts.text: Can you find the thickness of a single page of your notebook or textbook using a scale? Think of a way and write it. Carry out the activity and report your result.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Collect fallen leaves from the same tree. Identify the name of the tree whose leaves you have taken. Measure length and breadth of all these leaves using a 15-cm scale, as shown in Fig. 5.20. Record your observations in the Table 5.7.
texts.text: Collect fallen leaves from the same tree. Identify the name of the tree whose leaves you have taken. Measure length and breadth of all these leaves using a 15-cm scale, as shown in Fig. 5.20. Record your observations in the Table 5.7.

texts.text: Fig. 5.21: A maze

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Discuss with elders in your community what units were used for measurement of length in the olden days. Also, using the internet, try to find out about the length scales found in excavations of archaeological sites in India.
texts.text: Discuss with elders in your community what units were used for measurement of length in the olden days. Also, using the internet, try to find out about the length scales found in excavations of archaeological sites in India.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: a4 Create amaze using lines of 1 cm, 2cmand their combination. Part of it has been made for you in Fig. 5.21. Now use your imagination and expand it to a size as big as you want.
texts.text: a4 Create amaze using lines of 1 cm, 2cmand their combination. Part of it has been made for you in Fig. 5.21. Now use your imagination and expand it to a size as big as you want.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: How tall am I? Stand along a wall and with the help of an adult, mark your height (Fig. 5.22). Repeat it every three months to maintain a height record for yourself and your siblings.
texts.text: How tall am I? Stand along a wall and with the help of an adult, mark your height (Fig. 5.22). Repeat it every three months to maintain a height record for yourself and your siblings.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Let us design a fun method for measuring the distance between two places by using a bicycle. Attach a flexible metal strip to the spoke of the front wheel in such a manner that it hits the frame of the bicycle holding the wheel, every time it crosses it and produces a sound (Fig. 5.23).
texts.text: Let us design a fun method for measuring the distance between two places by using a bicycle. Attach a flexible metal strip to the spoke of the front wheel in such a manner that it hits the frame of the bicycle holding the wheel, every time it crosses it and produces a sound (Fig. 5.23).

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 5.22: Measuring height
texts.text: Fig. 5.22: Measuring height

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Now ride the bicycle slowly and count the number of times in which sound occurred. The number will give you the number of turns of your wheel made. Now measure the
texts.text: Now ride the bicycle slowly and count the number of times in which sound occurred. The number will give you the number of turns of your wheel made. Now measure the length of the outer boundary of the wheel using a string as done in Fig. 5.8. Multiply this length by the number of turns of the wheel. This is the distance you travelled.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 5.23: Measuring distance
texts.text: Fig. 5.23: Measuring distance

texts.orig: India has a rich history of measurement systems dating back to ancient times. Angula (finger width), multiples of angula, dhanusa, and yojana are some of the units mentioned in ancient Indian literature, and used in measuring artefacts, architecture, and
texts.text: India has a rich history of measurement systems dating back to ancient times. Angula (finger width), multiples of angula, dhanusa, and yojana are some of the units mentioned in ancient Indian literature, and used in measuring artefacts, architecture, and

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In some scales, you might have noticed another scale marking. This scale marking is in inches, where 1 inch = 2.54 cm. In earlier days, units, such as inch and foot, were used to measure length. These units are still used by some people.
texts.text: In some scales, you might have noticed another scale marking. This scale marking is in inches, where 1 inch = 2.54 cm. In earlier days, units, such as inch and foot, were used to measure length. These units are still used by some people.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If an object repeats its path after a fixed interval of time, its motion is said to be periodic. When an object is in circular motion, it moves along the circular path again and again. An object in oscillatory motion also repeats its motion while moving to and fro. Both circular and oscillatory motion are periodic in nature.
texts.text: If an object repeats its path after a fixed interval of time, its motion is said to be periodic. When an object is in circular motion, it moves along the circular path again and again. An object in oscillatory motion also repeats its motion while moving to and fro. Both circular and oscillatory motion are periodic in nature.
